question.text: Scenario: Karan Singh is advising a manufacturing company that exports automotive components. The following economic changes have occurred simultaneously: (1) RBI has cut the repo rate by 75 basis points over the last 6 months from 6.50% to 5.75%. (2) The Indian Rupee has depreciated 8% against the US Dollar from Rs.82 to Rs.88.50. (3) Global crude oil prices have fallen 25% from $90 to $67.50 per barrel. (4) India's GDP growth has improved from 5.5% to 7.0%. (5) The US Federal Reserve has started cutting rates, weakening the US Dollar globally. (6) WPI inflation has fallen from 6.5% to 3.2%. The company earns 60% of its revenue from exports (invoiced in USD) and imports 30% of its raw materials (also priced in USD). The remaining revenue is domestic, and 70% of raw materials are sourced domestically. Karan needs to assess the net impact on the company. Question 2 of 5: What is the net impact of INR depreciation on the company's revenue, given 60% export revenue in USD?

answer.text: Revenue will decrease due to weaker rupee
Export revenue in INR will increase as each USD now converts to more rupees, boosting total revenue
No impact as the company hedges all currency risk
Revenue will remain exactly the same

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The company earns 60% in USD. When INR depreciates from Rs.82 to Rs.88.50 (8%), each dollar of export revenue converts to 8% more rupees. This significantly boosts INR revenue from exports. **The Trap:** Depreciation helps exporters (more INR per USD). Only the import cost (30% of raw materials) is negatively affected. Net effect is positive since exports > imports. **Cross-Exam Context:** Currency impact on exporters tested in NISM Series XV and I. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For this company: 60% revenue gains 8% from depreciation while only 30% of costs increases by 8%. Net revenue impact is strongly positive.
